data.title: UnitedHealth: The Best House In A Broken Neighbourhood
data.description:
Thesis Summary

UnitedHealth Group (UNH) has been hard hit after earnings

Medicare has proven to be a real headwind, with high utilisation and reimbursement growth outpacing medical cost inflation.

Nonetheless, I still see a silver lining.

Yes, Medicare Advantage has become a challenge, and healthcare companies like UNH have been caught in the crossfire.

Nonetheless, I still see UNH as the insurer best positioned to survive this drawdown.

I reiterate a Strong Buy rating, doubling down on what I said in my last article, as UNH looks better positioned than competitors to ride this storm.

UNH Q4 Earnings

UnitedHealth’s latest earnings disappointed on guidance, which reinforced the concerns investors already had.

As we can see above, revenue has been flat over the last year, and the company has faced near term margin pressure too.

The stock has sold off accordingly as future expectations have been revised lower.

The key issue here is that CMS released its 2027 advance rate notice, pointing to an expected average revenue increase of just 0.09%, which represents a considerable slowdown from roughly 5% in 2026.

This all but guarantees that Medicare Advantage margins will compress and UNH earnings will take a hit.

As a response to this, UNH management has vowed to double down on cost-cutting and also reduce exposure to the less profitable segments.

Hopefully, with some restructuring, AI investment, and scale advantages, earnings may see some recovery in 2027.

Could Trump Double Back On Medicare?

The core problem facing health insurers today is simple arithmetic.

Reimbursements are flat, with the current administration taking a stance on healthcare affordability, but medical costs keep rising, rather fast.

Utilisation rates remain elevated, and drug costs continue to rise, but the CMS is no longer providing the cushion it once did.

This is not a UnitedHealth-specific problem, but rather a system-wide recalibration.

But could we see a reversal in this policy?

So far, President Donald Trump has been quick to reverse course on many issues, and this could also be the case here.

This is especially true if we see continued losses in the healthcare industry.

Why UnitedHealth Is Still Best Positioned?

UnitedHealth has scale in its favour, and much more.

Medicare-related products account for roughly two-thirds of the company’s premium revenue when including Medicare Advantage and other segments.

And then we have Optum.

While traditional insurers simply reimburse claims, UnitedHealth increasingly manages care pathways, aligns incentives with providers, and uses data to reduce unnecessary utilisation.

In a hostile one-market like the one we have today, this differentiation could become life-saving.

Smaller or less integrated Medicare Advantage organisations are already pulling out of geographies, cutting benefits, or shrinking enrollment to protect capital.

If UNH can endure the storm, then it can pick up this business in the future.

Historically, heavily regulated industries follow this pattern

Policy tightens, then margins compress, which forces weak players to exit, and the survivors consolidate the market.

UnitedHealth has the balance sheet, data, and operating leverage to play the long game.

Valuation

The forward multiple reflects a business facing margin pressure, slower earnings growth, and regulatory uncertainty.

Yes, the business is clearly being challenged, but that doesn’t change the fact that UNH is a high-quality cash generation machine, with sustainable dividends and a proven track record.

While returns may not be incredible in the next year, this is an easy stock to buy and hold for the long term.

Risks

There is a very real chance that the stock remains “dead money” for some time.

We don’t really have a lot of near-term catalysts, and a recovery will take time.

Investors looking for a quick bounce tied to April’s final rate announcement may be disappointed, and even earnings recovery in the next few quarters might not be enough to shift sentiment.

Final Thoughts

The healthcare industry is facing structural pressure, but UNH, thanks to its scale and integration, has good odds of surviving.

This is a stock to own if you believe that, when the dust settles, the largest and best-equipped insurer will emerge with a stronger competitive position than it has today.

Sometimes the best investment is simply the one that can endure the storm.

data.title: Boston Beer: A Debt-Free Craft And Beyond Beer Leader Priced At A Deep Discount
data.description:
Introduction & Financials

The Boston Beer Company (SAM) has been very volatile in recent years, with the hard seltzer mania’s boom and bust being followed by a period of weakness in the general consumer environment, although the stock is now at a point where it’s significantly undervalued, offering an opportunity to get a piece of this craft beer (and beyond through hard tea, seltzer, and canned cocktails) leader that’s in a solid position to benefit from long-term premiumization trends.

SAM’s Q3 was solid overall, with EPS beating the market’s expectations significantly, while the company’s free cash flow reached $193.5 million over the first 9 months of 2025.

SAM’s latest guidance now sees a 1% to 2% increase in prices, while capital spending is expected to drop to $50 million to $70 million (compared to the prior guidance of $70 million to $90 million) while their advertising and promotion spending would grow significantly to $50 million to $60 million (vs. $30 million to $50 million), while they estimate the impact of tariffs at a lower $9 million to $13 million now and also noting that Q4 is generally their weakest quarter in terms of revenue and margin. Regarding tariffs, the company’s CFO highlighted the following during the Q3 Earnings Call:

The change to our tariff estimate is due to lower-than-anticipated tariffs primarily on material source from Canada and exempt from the tariffs as a U.S. MC compliant goods. In the first 9 months, we have incurred $7.1 million in tariff costs.

As they reiterated in their Q3 call, Q4 is often weak, which is also visible in 2024, with only ~16.8% of their cash from operations coming in Q4 2024, while their free cash flow reached $171.62 million in 2024, $201.06 million in 2023 and $109.36 million in 2022, with the latter being hit by a mix of high CAPEX and negative working capital changes.

Financially, based on SAM’s latest report, we see an excellent position, with the company’s current assets covering their total liabilities very well, while they have zero debt and ~$250.45 million worth of cash and cash equivalents available, providing significant financial flexibility.

Although SAM doesn’t pay a dividend, the company has so far repurchased $161 million worth of shares from December 30, 2024 to October 17, 2025, which annualizes to about $202 million, for a buyback yield of roughly 8.86% based on today’s prices, which is very significant in this market and should be covered by their free cash flow. As a note, they have about $266 million remaining on the $1.6 billion share repurchase authorization as of October 17.

SAM is expected to release their Q4 Earnings on February 11, with relatively weak EPS and revenue estimates (as they mention, that’s normal), and I believe the report will carry more importance since the company is likely going to present an initial 2026 guidance, which can cause a lot of volatility even if they beat the market’s estimates during the final quarter. Especially given the seasonality of the business, I wouldn't be discouraged by a weak Q4 in terms of free cash flow, EPS or revenue, and instead I'll pay close attention to their plans for this year and potential expectations of more margin improvements, as they continue expanding their advertising efforts.

Risks & Opportunities

Right now, the beer/alcoholic beverage industry is affected by the classic de-premiumization that’s generally hurting the broader market, as consumers have to adapt to the economy, although the long-term trend is quite the opposite, with clear premiumization trends being visible as the modern consumer puts more importance on the quality rather than the quantity of alcohol they consume.

Ultimately, this trade-down trend has been visible in recent years across several industries, from auto parts and even to food, with the US consumer being characterized by higher levels of stress going into 2026, having to deal with growing costs of living and pressured discretionary budgets, all of which are ultimately reflected in the purchasing behavior.

There’s also data showing that new generations tend to drink less alcohol, although I believe we need more data, as the past half a decade has been significantly affected by the ongoing macro pressure while costs went up and budgets fell, although the quality over quantity trend is clear, and brewers are already adapting and developing a variety of options, from craft to zero-alcohol, gluten-free or even flavored beer, among others, which the company also commented about during their Q3 Earnings Call:

Now turning to the current industry environment. I mentioned on our last call that we were experiencing a challenging macroeconomic environment. And those trends continued into the third quarter. Economic uncertainty that has consumers more tightly managing their budgets (...) Despite these current industry headwinds, we continue to see long-term growth opportunities in the beyond beer category, also known as the fourth category. Beyond beer represents more than 85% of our volume. We believe that the beyond beer category share will grow as the drinker is younger and more diverse than traditional beer. Our brands are well positioned to participate in this growth and our strong innovation culture allows us to move quickly to add to the portfolio as consumer trends evolve. The latest example is Sun Cruiser, which was one of the top volume gainers in RTD Spirits so far this year.

At the same time, although the high-rate environment doesn’t really impact SAM directly since they have zero debt, it can still hurt the environment they play in and ultimately impact their consumers for longer, while the market’s fear can even result in pressure on their valuation as they could be adjusted to the “new environment” they operate in.

On top of that, we have renewed risks of tariffs on Canada following Trump’s threat of 100% tariffs as a result of the country’s deal with China, which can add another layer of uncertainty that the company will likely comment on in their Q4 call.

Still, SAM should be in a position to enjoy potential catalysts for alcohol consumption in 2026, including the FIFA World Cup that will take place during the summer of 2026, America’s 250th celebration or even the Winter Olympics or the Super Bowl soon.

Meanwhile, given their size and excellent financial position with strong cash flow and brands, SAM could even be the target of an acquisition, and the market also sees the potential of a rise in M&A activity in 2026, for which I believe SAM would be an optimal target given their size, price and especially quality, which offers a safety net in this case.

Risks Reiterated

Although the company remains in an excellent financial position and is able to make a lot of money now, they will still depend on the consumer, and even if their brands can stand to benefit from premiumization and other long-term trends, the near-term can continue to be very volatile.

Meanwhile, the environment can continue to deteriorate, with the renewed fears around tariffs and the general economy potentially leading to a more careful approach from the market, which can lead to pressure on their margins and ultimately the valuation.

Valuation

For SAM’s DCF to equity valuation, to take into account the company’s new CAPEX and tariff guidance, as well as the weaker results expected in Q4, I’ll assume a base free cash flow of $210 million in 2025. To be more conservative, I’ll go for a CAGR of only 8% over the first 5 years, (which is significantly below the market’s EPS expectations) to take into account the near-term demand pressure and competition, offset by the potentially improving environment, long-term tailwinds and significant buybacks impacting the final per share results, followed by a 5% CAGR for the subsequent 5 years and a 2.5% terminal growth rate, with a discount rate of 12% to be even more conservative and take into account the higher volatility and a stronger margin of safety, with 10.83 million shares outstanding as of the Q3 report.

This way, we get to an equity value of $3.04 billion, for an intrinsic value of $280.25, which is well above the current $218.40 at the moment of writing this, providing a strong margin of safety given the conservative assumptions used.

Conclusions

I rate SAM a Strong Buy now, with excellent financials, zero debt and strong brands that should be able to benefit from long-term premiumization tailwinds, despite the near-term weakness in the environment. There’s certainly more room for downside if the weakness in the environment continues, but the overall risk-reward seems to be greatly in their favor.

data.title: Third Avenue Real Estate Value Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Dear Fellow Shareholders,

We are pleased to provide you with the Third Avenue Real Estate Value Fund’s (the “Fund”) report for the quarter ended December 31, 2025. For the most recent calendar year, the Fund generated a return of +11.61% (after fees). In comparison, the Fund’s most relevant benchmark, the MSCI ACWI IMI Core Real Estate Index¹, generated a return of +9.86% (before fees) over the same time period.

The primary contributors to the Fund’s performance this quarter included investments in National Storage REIT (a leading owner of self-storage facilities in Australia that agreed to be taken private) and several real estate investment trusts (“REITs”) involved with industrial and logistics real estate (Prologis, First Industrial, and Segro plc). Notwithstanding, these gains were offset by detractors in the period, including the Fund’s investments in U.S.-based homebuilders (Lennar Corp., PulteGroup, and D.R. Horton) and certain U.K.-based property companies (Berkeley Group and Unite Group plc). Further insights into these enterprises, as well as Fund’s most recent addition (FirstService Corp.), are included herein.

Despite the differentiated performance this year, Fund Management considers the Fund’s long-term results the most relevant scorecard. To that end, the Third Avenue Real Estate Value Fund has generated an annualized return of +8.96% (after fees) since its inception in 1998. This performance indicates that an initial investment of $100,000 in the Fund would have a market value exceeding $1,000,000 at year-end (with distributions reinvested)—or more than the same $100,000 would be worth had it been placed into a passive mutual fund tracking the benchmark over the same time period.

Activity

This year marks four decades of operations for Third Avenue Management (“Third Avenue” or “the Firm”). In Fund Management’s opinion, such a milestone demonstrates the Firm’s commitment to its core principles, as well as a willingness to adapt in its pursuit of long-term wealth creation for Third Avenue clients.

For instance, the Firm has refined its product offering to focus on an expanded set of core competencies, while also strengthening certain aspects of the investment and portfolio construction process over the years. At the same time though, Third Avenue’s distinct approach to value investing has largely endured. In fact, the Firm seems more resolute than ever in targeting well-capitalized enterprises with discounted securities and prospects to compound capital over time. To wit, each Third Avenue Mutual Fund strategy was recognized by Morningstar with a 5-star Overall Rating at year-end. (See important disclosures below).

Other hallmarks that have persisted include the Funds having little overlap with broad-based indices, as well as the passive funds that track such composites. In addition, Third Avenue’s portfolios tend to be prudently concentrated around a “select-set” of opportunities, presenting prospects for discernible outcomes over time. The holdings also seem unconventional in other respects, most notably as well-capitalized entities that frequently engage in “resource conversion”—a term Third Avenue has coined to describe corporate actions intended to surface value, including privatizations, mergers, spin-offs, special dividends, share repurchases, and strategic investments.

Coincidentally, the recent quarter was one of the most active periods of resource conversion for the Real Estate Value Fund holdings in many years. As a matter of fact, more than one-third of the underlying portfolio engaged in (or announced) such initiatives during the period, with some of the most notable including:

  • National Storage REIT (NTSGF), a leading owner, manager, and developer of self-storage facilities in Australia agreeing to a $4.0 billion AUD privatization transaction with entities (and funds) affiliated with Brookfield Asset Management and GIC. The well-executed agreement not only reflects a 25% premium to the price prevailing for National Storage common stock before the announcement, but it also includes a modest premium to the company’s Net Asset Value (“NAV”) for “control”.
  • Rayonier (RYN), one of the largest owners of timberlands in the U.S. completing the divestiture of its New Zealand venture, paying a special dividend, and announcing a merger with industry peer PotlatchDeltic. Once completed, the combined company will benefit from additional efficiencies within its timber segment, in Fund Management’s opinion, as well as more scale in its real estate and resources portfolio that includes substantial sub-surface rights in the Smackover Formation.
  • Brookfield Corp. (BN), a leading investor and manager of real assets globally announcing the acquisition of the remaining 26% of Oaktree Capital in conjunction with an affiliate (Brookfield Asset Management). In addition, the company (i) announced that certain subsidiaries entered an $80 billion strategic partnership with the U.S. government to build a new fleet of nuclear factories and (ii) indicated that it intends to recapitalize its Center Parcs hospitality platform for nearly $6 billion, surfacing value on both fronts.
  • Lennar Corp. (LEN), the second largest homebuilder in the U.S. completed an exchange offer for its remaining 20% stake in Millrose Properties, a “land banking” company that Lennar spun-out earlier in the year. The transaction effectively acted as an accelerated share repurchase, with Lennar exchanging the Millrose shares for 5% of its outstanding A shares. Not only does this deal move Lennar closer to a “land light” model, but it also seems to enhance the company’s long-term earnings potential on a per-share basis.
  • Fidelity National Financial (FNF), the largest provider of title insurance for residential and commercial properties in the U.S. distributing a near $500 million stake in its life insurance affiliate F&G Annuities and Life (“F&G”). The transaction will increase the public float of the separately listed entity, with the aim of improving (i) F&G’s cost of capital (i.e., implied multiple) relative to other market comparables in this line of insurance and (ii) the “sum of the parts” value for Fidelity National when also factoring in its other distinctive title insurance and transactions businesses.
  • Prologis (PLD), the largest owner of modern logistics and industrial properties globally sponsoring a REIT that was listed in China to focus on investing in strategic properties within the “Greater Bay” region. The offering further expands the $65 billion investment management platform at Prologis, which is likely to scale up more in the year ahead, alongside the company’s focus on unlocking data center opportunities alongside institutional partners.

In parallel to these developments, the Fund initiated a position in the common stock of another enterprise that is well-versed in resource conversion: FirstService Corporation (FSV) (“FirstService”).

Founded in 1989, FirstService is a Canadian-based real estate services company with two key business segments: FirstService Residential and FirstService Brands. Through the residential segment, the company is the largest provider of property management and ancillary services for residential communities in North America, with a particular focus on serving condominiums, homeowner’s associations (“HOA’s”), and master planned communities. FirstService also has a well-established presence within its brands segment, where the company is a leading provider of services for commercial properties, including essential functions such as restoration, roofing, and fire protection.

In addition to controlling these strategic platforms, FirstService meets the Real Estate team’s key investment criteria. That is to say, the company is very conservatively capitalized with a net-debt-to-asset ratio of less than 10%, a fixed-charge coverage ratio of nearly 7.0 times, and ample free cash flow with minimal “capex”. FirstService is also very well-managed and run by an aligned control group that shares a long-term view. In fact, insiders own approximately 10% of the company, and the team has compounded book value per share by 20% per year over the past 10 years, including dividends.

Despite those attributes, FirstService common trades at implied multiples that haven’t been obtainable in a decade, as well as ones that seem quite modest for such resilient businesses (e.g., the renewal rate on the residential management contracts is greater than 95%). Notwithstanding, Fund Management recognizes that the company has experienced an “earnings shortfall” in its restoration segment without any hurricanes making landfall during 2025—an occurrence that any decent actuary would likely avoid underwriting into perpetuity.

Therefore, Fund Management believes that over the medium-term FirstService is likely to: (i) further expand its residential business, with 80% of single-family homes now being built in HOA communities, (ii) make additional “tuck-in” acquisitions within its brand verticals, including the accretive buyout of certain franchisees, and (iii) return to more “normalized” levels of profitability within restoration. In combination, these items should lead to an improved cost of capital. If not, it would not be inconceivable for FirstService to engage in some sort of resource conversion, potentially even splitting off a segment similar to when the company separated Colliers International Group (a commercial real estate brokerage and services firm) in 2015.

Outside of this addition, the Fund’s activity was modest in nature. The other primary changes included the Fund adding to issuers where the price-to-value gap widened, including the Unite Group (U.K. student housing), Fidelity National Financial, and AMH Inc. (U.S. single-family rentals). The Fund also trimmed back CBRE Group and JLL Inc. (global real estate services businesses) alongside recent performance, received a special dividend from Rayonier, and extended the Fund’s British Pound hedge.

Positioning

Following this activity, the Fund had 40.3% of its capital invested in U.S.-based companies focused on Residential Real Estate, including those involved with: Homebuilding (Lennar Corp., D.R. Horton, PulteGroup, and Champion Homes); Niche Rental Platforms (Sun Communities and AMH); Land and Timber (Five Point, Rayonier, and Weyerhaeuser); and Mortgage and Title Insurance (Fannie Mae, Freddie Mac, and Fidelity National). In Fund Management’s view, each enterprise has a well-established position in the residential value chain and is supported by fundamental drivers, including: (i) near-record low levels of for-sale inventories, (ii) near-record high demand for affordable product, and (iii) industry dynamics favoring scaled players over time.

An additional 27.5% of the Fund’s capital is invested in North American-based companies involved with Commercial Real Estate, including: Real Estate Services (CBRE Group, JLL, and FirstService); Asset Management (Brookfield Corp.); Industrial and Logistics (Prologis, First Industrial, and Wesco); and Self-Storage (U-Haul Holdings). In Fund Management’s opinion, these holdings represent platforms that would be very difficult to reassemble. They also comprise select pockets of commercial real estate that seemingly favor long-term investors with (i) structural demand drivers, (ii) limited maintenance “capex”, and (iii) prospects to “self-finance” value-enhancing initiatives.

The Fund also had 27.6% of its capital invested in International Real Estate companies. These businesses are largely focused on the same types of activities as outlined above, simply with leading platforms in their respective regions. At quarter-end, these included companies involved with: Commercial Real Estate (Big Yellow, CK Asset, National Storage, Jardine Matheson, Segro, and Wharf); Residential Real Estate (Berkeley, Unite Group, and Ingenia); and Real Estate Services (Savills and Accor). The holdings are also listed in developed markets where Fund Management believes there are (i) adequate disclosures and securities laws and (ii) ample opportunities for change of control transactions (i.e., the U.K., Australia, France, Hong Kong, and Singapore).

The remaining 4.6% of the Fund’s capital is in Cash, Debt & Options. These holdings include U.S.-Dollar based cash and equivalents, short-term U.S. Treasuries, and hedges relating to certain foreign currency exposures (Hong Kong Dollar and British Pound).

The Fund’s allocations across these segments and geographies are outlined below. It also remains Fund Management’s opinion that when viewed in the aggregate, the portfolio continues to represent “strategic real estate at value prices”. Put otherwise, the Fund’s holdings include some of the premier real estate platforms globally, while trading at more than a 20% discount to our estimates of NAV at year-end—nearly twice the long-term average.

F‍und Commentary

In the 2025 edition of The Land Trap: A New History of the World’s Oldest Asset Class, up-and-coming financial author Mike Bird dives into the history of real estate, as well as the role land has played in various regions over time. Within the work, he also details that real estate remains the “world’s largest single asset class by some magnitude” with McKinsey & Co. estimating that land, residential, and commercial property account for approximately two-thirds of “real wealth” globally. Additionally, Bird spells out the “three critical factors” that explain why land is “still such a store of enormous wealth” including its “fixed-amount, immobility, and lack of material decay”.

Fund Management shared similar observations within this recent feature on real estate securities: A Strategic Roadmap for Listed Real Estate. The Third Avenue team noted other potential benefits of investing in real estate, including the prospects to generate current yield and capital appreciation, as well as gain inflation attributes and portfolio diversification.

That said, Bird largely skipped over the transformation of listed real estate in recent decades—thus democratizing the asset class that he seems to suggest is often unattainable. In fact, if Bird were to have investigated further, he would have found that there are now more than $6 trillion of publicly traded real estate companies that investors can access globally. Not only that, Bird would have likely found that investing in real estate through the public markets has certain advantages, including having provided superior outcomes relative to private real estate vehicles over various periods of time, as outlined in this study: REITs Outperform Private Real Estate by More Than 2.0% in DB Plans.

Bird is certainly not at fault for overlooking this angle though, as a disproportionate share of “fund flows” into real estate in recent years have also skewed towards private real estate. As a matter of fact, Citigroup estimates that $97 billion of capital was invested into private U.S. REIT vehicles from January 2020 through December 2024. Whereas real estate focused funds in the listed space in the U.S. experienced more than $35 billion of net redemptions over the same period.

Insofar as Fund Management can gather, the primary driver of this shift has been the desire to mitigate “volatility” through private funds, as listed real estate strategies are essentially “marked-to-market” daily. Nonetheless, it is a trend that is on the verge of reversing in the second half of this decade, in Fund Management’s opinion, for two primary reasons:

  1. A lack of volatility doesn’t always lead to superior investment outcomes. The most recent example of which includes a large private real estate fund electing in December 2025 to convert from a privately traded fund to a publicly traded “closed end” fund to meet redemption requests, only to have the shares begin trading at more than a 35% discount to the previous “appraised value”. While the initial years may have provided a “smooth” experience for the investors in this fund, recent events seem more testing as covered by Jason Zweig in this Wall Street Journal article: When Your Private Fund Turns $1 Into 60 Cents.
  2. Valuations for listed real estate are compelling on both a relative and absolute basis. As Fund Management also alluded to in the Strategic Roadmap, broader equity markets (as measured by the S&P 500 Index) imply record high multiples on most fundamental metrics (e.g., price to earnings multiples, price to sales, etc.). On the other hand, listed real estate in the U.S. was trading at a historically wide discount to broader equities on traditional cash flow metrics at the end of the year (e.g., price to earnings, funds from operations, etc.) as well as a 10% discount to liquidation value, per Green Street Advisors, when viewed on an equal-weight basis.

Given these dynamics, Fund Management believes the next five years could end up being analogous to the early 2000s—a period when compelling valuations for listed real estate in the U.S. led to a multi-year period of net inflows, as well as vastly differentiated returns relative to broader equities. Such a path is even more likely, in our view, for global real estate strategies that have the flexibility to include international allocations, where the price-to-value disconnect is more pronounced.

In fact, should these outcomes materialize, the period ahead may prove similar to the early 2000s in another respect: it was amongst the most rewarding times to be invested in listed real estate, as well as the Third Avenue Real Estate Value Fund. It might also compel Bird to highlight the evolution of real estate securities in the next edition of The Land Trap, as well as the various opportunities in listed real estate available for all types of investors.

We thank you for your continued support and look forward to writing to you again next quarter.

Sincerely,

The Third Avenue Real Estate Value Team

Risks that could negatively impact returns include: overbuilding and increased competition, increases in property taxes and operating expenses, lack of financing, vacancies, environmental contamination and its related clean-up, changes in interest rates, casualty or condemnation losses, and variations in rental income.

The Morningstar Rating™ for funds, or “star rating,” is calculated for mutual funds, variable annuity and variable life subaccounts, exchange-traded funds, closed-end funds, and separate accounts) with at least a three-year history. Exchange-traded funds and open-ended mutual funds are considered a single population for comparative purposes. It is calculated based on a Morningstar Risk-Adjusted Return measure that accounts for variation in a managed product’s monthly excess performance, placing more emphasis on downward variations and rewarding consistent performance. The top 10% of products in each product category receive 5 stars, the next 22.5% receive 4 stars, the next 35% receive 3 stars, the next 22.5% receive 2 stars, and the bottom 10% receive 1 star. data.title: AngloGold Ashanti: 'Gold Is The Money Of Kings,' Be A King
data.description:
There is an old anonymous saying that "gold is the money of kings." Recently, the big rally in gold likely has a lot of investors feeling like kings. However, as J.P. Morgan once said, "Nothing so undermines your financial judgment as the sight of your neighbor getting rich." The moral of that quote is that, given the bull market in gold, it pays to be wary of "herd mentality." That is especially given that there are multiple ways to invest in gold: you can buy minted gold coins, gold bars, funds that invest in gold miners and streamers, or individual gold stocks - each path has differing risks. Yet, as part of a well-diversified portfolio, for years I've advised investors on Seeking Alpha to own an allocation to gold as a critical hedge against inflation and geopolitical risks. In my opinion, the latter is currently dominating the macro-environment and has been driving gold higher. I don't see that catalyst abating any time soon. In fact, if the last year is any indication, it could get even worse going forward. But we need to be prudent here and, perhaps, curb our enthusiasm a bit.

Today, I'll take a look at AngloGold Ashanti (AU) to see if it might still be a good vehicle to hop on for the historic bull run that we are all currently living in. AU - the stock, not the element in the periodic table - has certainly participated:

As you can see from the chart, over the past year AngloGold Ashanti has just ripped higher, significantly outperforming two of my favorite precious metals investments - the Van Eck Junior Gold Miners ETF (GDXJ) and Pan American Silver (PAAS) - see "PAAS: Get Ready For A Potential Q4 Blowout" - and just clobbering the broad market equity indexes as represented by the S&P500 (VOO), the Nasdaq-100 (QQQ), and the DJIA (DIA). AngloGold, being highly levered to the price of gold, has outperformed the price of gold as represented by the (GLD) ETF by 3.5x.

This chart is exactly why I have advised investors to always have an allocation to gold and silver (mostly gold), and in my opinion, the higher your net worth, the larger your percentage allocation should be. That's because you have more to lose by fiat currency devaluation due to either inflation, the Federal debt level, or unwise political policy making (or all of the above, as I would argue is the current case).

With that as background, let's take a look at AngloGold Ashanti to see if, despite the bull run in the stock over the past year, it remains a good way to invest in gold.

Investment Thesis

The slide below was taken from AngloGold's presentation at the Mining Forum Americas 2025 Presentation last September:

As you can see from the graphic, a geographically diverse set of "Tier-1" mines accounts for the majority of AU's production and the vast majority of its gold reserves. The company describes a "Tier-1" mine as one that has a low AISC cost, is large-scale, and has excellent growth potential moving forward. The company also has a set of "Tier-2" mines that, while they cannot match the profitability of the Tier-1 mines, are dependable generators of cash and have opportunities to improve margin. That said, in the current bull market in gold, even AngloGold's "Tier-2" mines are knocking the ball out of the park.

According to investingnews.com and based on full-year 2024 results, AngloGold is the #6 global producer of gold. The site reports that AngloGold's largest wholly owned operation is the Geita mine in Northwest Tanzania. Geita consists of multiple open-pit and underground operations, which in aggregate produced 483,000 ounces of gold in FY2024.

Despite the rapid rise of the stock price over the past year, AU trades with a rather subdued TTM P/E=24x and a forward P/E=19.9x. Currently, AU pays a $2.53/share dividend that is good enough for a 2.3% yield. But it is a variable dividend (see below). This is the primary investment opportunity AU presents: a gold-based investment that is actually trading at a substantial discount to the broad equity market as represented by the S&P 500 (P/E=31.5x, yield=1.1%).

Meantime, the U.S. dollar continues to weaken, as evidenced by this chart of the DXY U.S. dollar index:

As you can see from the graphic, the DXY is down 10%+ over the past year and took another plunge lower last week after President Trump's speech at Davos, in which he questioned European countries' commitment to the NATO alliance even as it was he himself who was threatening a fellow founding NATO member, Denmark, over his desire to annex Greenland. I would argue that Trump's arguably erratic and chaotic tariff war - which he also used to threaten NATO members last week in Davos if they didn't back his Greenland itinerary - has been another big factor in U.S. dollar weakness. To sum it up, it is what many investors consider to be the loss of American Exceptionalism, a loss in the faith of a Trump-led America, and the massive uncertainty that current American foreign and tariff policies have injected into the global macro-environment that has arguably ushered in a global movement in "U.S. dollar debasement." In turn, the very weak U.S. dollar has clearly driven a flight to quality, and that means into gold.

With that as background and perhaps a motivating factor, let's take a closer look at AngloGold Ashanti.

Production

The slide below shows AU's 2025 full-year production guidance and cost profile:

As you can see from the graphic, the midpoint of FY25 guidance equates to 3,062,000 million oz of gold production, with the midpoint of ASIC being $1,642/oz. With gold trading at $5,200/oz at pixel time, AU is obviously generating very strong free-cash-flow.

Indeed, in the Q3 earnings report, the company generated $920 million of free-cash-flow (+140% yoy) and an estimated $1.85/share based on the 496.4 million shares outstanding at the end of the quarter:

In Q3, AngloGold's AISC price was $1,766/oz, while the average realized selling price was $3,490/oz.

Going Forward

In the Q3 report, AngloGold reaffirmed the FY2025 guidance presented earlier in this article. The Q4 and full-year results are scheduled to be released on February 20, and here are the current consensus analyst estimates courtesy of Yahoo Finance:

As you can see from the graphic, the current Q4 consensus earnings estimate of $1.86/share would be +109% yoy. The full-year FY25 earnings estimate of $5.50/share would be +149% yoy, while the full-year FY2026 estimate of $8.86 would be +61% sequentially. Q4 revenue is expected to hit $3 billion, which would be +74.4% yoy.

If AU were to hit the $1.86/share estimate, that would be +41% sequentially as compared to the $1.32/share earned in Q3.

Not bad for a company that has such a strong free-cash-flow profile and is trading with a TTM P/E of only 24x, right?

Organic growth opportunities moving forward include continued ramp-up of the Obuasi Mine (gold production up 30% yoy in Q3 to 69,000 oz) and the completion of the Augusta Gold acquisition in Nevada, and specifically the world-class Arthur Mine, which in the Q3 presentation AngloGold said the Nevada Arthur Gold Project was "one of the largest greenfield discoveries of the century in the United States."

Indeed, on the Q3 conference call, AngloGold CEO Alberto Calderon commented on excellent progress in Nevada and Arthur and the potential of the play:

Now we move to Nevada. While North Bullfrog is our first step in Nevada after the Tier 1 discovery at which the Nevada strategy turns. It is one of the most significant gold discoveries in the generation and is in one of the world's top mining jurisdictions. This is not a modest asset. It's large with significant mine grade. As of our latest update, Arthur holds a resource of around 16 million ounces. The deposits are predominantly oxide, which is key. Our focus is currently on the Merlin deposit, where we have some more exceptionally high-grade intercepts during resource definition drilling.

The Dividend

The Q3 report included this dividend announcement:

An interim dividend of 91 US cents per share was declared for Q3 2025, which includes the minimum quarterly dividend of $63m or 12.5 US cents, with the balance reflecting the decision to pay half of free cash flow generated for the three months ended 30 September 2025. While AngloGold Ashanti's dividend policy commits to this 'true up' payment to 50% of free cash flow annually at year-end, the Board used its discretion to make the payment at the quarter given the strength of cash flows and its confidence in the outlook for the balance of the year.

As you can see from the quote, AngloGold pays a minimum quarterly dividend of $0.125/share but also has a variable dividend "kicker" based on 50% of free-cash-flow (with Board discretion). In my opinion, this is a very bullish backdrop going into the Q4 earnings report, given the rapid appreciation of the value of gold during Q4 as compared to Q3 (see chart below, red annotations added by the author) and knowing that gold production was weighted toward the 2H of the year:

It would be difficult to precisely estimate what AU's Q4 free-cash-flow and dividend might be given the wide range of production guidance and the unknown of the average realized price, but I think it safe to say that the Q4 dividend declaration is likely to be significantly higher than the Q3 "all-in" dividend of $0.91/share because the variable portion of the dividend is likely to go up given the chart above and the obvious impact of the price of gold on AU's ultimate free-cash-flow generation. After all, YTD through Q3, AngloGold has paid its shareholders $927 million in dividends as compared to $439 million in dividends for all of 2025.

Risks

An international miner like AngloGold faces local political risks as well as all the risks involved with being a mining operator of multiple mines spread across the globe. The majority of AU's gold production comes from Africa, which has a history of various political and societal disruptions from time-to-time:

Being a commodity producer, AngloGold is obviously extremely exposed to the price of the commodity itself: in this case, gold. A change in America's tariff, trade, social, and foreign policies could lead to a turnaround in the U.S. dollar and therefore push the price of gold down.

Alternatively, upside risks include a continuation of the weak U.S. dollar and the continuing appreciation in the price of gold.

AngloGold has a very strong balance sheet - with $2.55 billion in cash at the end of Q3, the net-debt-to-adjusted-EBITDA ratio was only 0.09x. S&P Global recently raised AngloGold's credit rating to positive from stable and reaffirmed its BB+ rating, stating:

We expect AngloGold Ashanti PLC's (AngloGold Ashanti's) earnings base to become structurally stronger over the next few years, underpinned by disciplined cost control and ongoing portfolio optimization, helping to narrow the unit cost gap relative to investment-grade peers.

I mentioned "herd mentality" earlier in this article, so it's notable to see how much love AU stock gets on Seeking Alpha and Wall Street (left side of graphic):

On the right-side of the graphic, the "Factor Grades," I take issue with the valuation rating of C+. While the valuation of AU may be high from a historic perspective, my opinion is that it does not adequately consider the massive increase in free-cash-flow this company is generating relative to its significant discount to the S&P 500. I am not trying to compare the operations of a miner like AngloGold with the average S&P 500 company; clearly that isn't a valid observation. I am simply comparing the valuation of AngloGold, the miner, with the valuation of the broad S&P 500, which is not growing free-cash-flow nearly as fast as AU.

Summary & Conclusion

AngloGold has a very strong and well-diversified gold production portfolio and a very strong free-cash-flow profile. Yet the company is currently trading at a significant discount to the S&P 500 despite having a superior growth profile and its potential for significant and superior dividend growth going forward.

In my opinion, AngloGold represents extraordinary value given its Tier-1 low-cost mines, its organic growth potential, and its low P/E valuation combined with my overall very bullish view on the future price of gold.

AngloGold is a STRONG BUY.

data.title: Why Midstream Cash Flows Hold Up When Energy Prices Don't
data.description:
Midstream energy investors have endured dramatic commodity swings over the past several years. Oil prices collapsed during the 2020 pandemic, surged to multi-year highs by 2022, and then moderated again. Natural gas followed a similar boom-and-bust pattern. For producers, these moves translated into volatile earnings and capital spending cycles. For midstream Master Limited Partnerships (MLPs), the outcome was notably different. Despite sharp price fluctuations, most pipeline and storage operators continued to generate relatively steady cash flow, reinforcing the defensive nature of the midstream business model.¹

Commodity Volatility Versus Throughput Stability

Severe commodity downturns can pressure midstream operators when low prices discourage drilling and reduce volumes. That dynamic was evident during the 2014–2016 energy downturn and again in 2020, when some pipelines experienced lower utilization. History also shows that production often rebounds once prices stabilize, even if prices remain below prior peaks. After crude prices retreated from 2022 highs, U.S. oil production still climbed to record levels as producers focused on efficiency and capital discipline.²

Natural gas markets tell a similar story. Even as benchmark gas prices fell from their 2022 spike, U.S. production reached record highs in 2023, supported by steady domestic demand and expanding LNG export capacity.³ For midstream operators, the volume of hydrocarbons moving through pipelines matters far more than spot prices. New export terminals, petrochemical plants, and power-generation demand effectively add new sources of throughput, helping sustain cash flow even in volatile pricing environments.

The Toll-Road Model in Practice

Midstream MLPs are often described as toll roads for the energy economy. Their revenues are largely generated from long-term, fee-based contracts tied to volumes rather than commodity prices. Many of these agreements include minimum volume commitments or take-or-pay provisions that provide baseline revenue even during periods of weaker production.⁴ Inflation-linked tariff escalators further support cash-flow stability over time.⁴

Demand fundamentals also reinforce resilience. Tens of millions of U.S. households rely on natural gas for heating and electricity generation, creating persistent utilization of pipeline infrastructure.⁵ As a result, midstream firms have generally exhibited lower earnings volatility than upstream producers, whose results are directly tied to price movements. Industry analysis consistently shows that fee-based midstream cash flows have remained comparatively stable through multiple commodity cycles.⁶

This insulation is not absolute. Extended price collapses can eventually reduce production volumes and pipeline utilization. However, compared with exploration and production companies or oilfield service firms, midstream assets have historically delivered more predictable cash generation due to their contractual structure and essential role in the energy system.

Outlook and Structural Improvements

The current midstream landscape is arguably more durable than in prior cycles. Many operators have reduced leverage, shifted toward self-funded capital spending, and emphasized balance sheet strength. Institutional asset managers note that these changes have improved the sector’s ability to withstand commodity volatility and interest rate shifts.⁷

Risks remain, including prolonged economic slowdowns, regulatory changes, or sustained commodity weakness. Rising interest rates can also pressure income-oriented valuations. Still, the underlying investment thesis remains intact. Midstream MLPs offer exposure to essential energy infrastructure with less direct sensitivity to commodity prices. In volatile markets, that combination of contractual revenue and steady demand continues to distinguish the sector.

Footnotes

  1. VettaFi, “Explaining Recent MLP and Midstream Weakness and the Outlook,” October 2023.

  2. John Kemp, “Record US Oil and Gas Production Keeps Prices Under Pressure,” Reuters, March 4, 2024.

  3. U.S. Energy Information Administration, “Five States Drove Record U.S. Natural Gas Production in 2023,” Today in Energy, 2024.

  4. Cohen & Steers, “Midstream Energy Infrastructure Overview,” 2024.

  5. U.S. Energy Information Administration, “Use of Natural Gas in the United States,” 2024.

  6. Invesco, “Midstream Energy Infrastructure: Overview and Benefits,” 2024.

  7. Advisor Perspectives, “Explaining MLP and Midstream Performance Through Market Cycles,” October 2025.

data.title: Meta: Set For New Highs In 2026
data.description:
Meta Platforms (META) crushed fourth-quarter earnings and revenue estimates on Wednesday due to a strong online shopping season that benefited the social media platform’s core advertising business. This caused shares of the enterprise to rise more than 6% in after-hours trading on Wednesday, which indicates a strong opening on Thursday as well.

Meta continued to maintain extreme free cash flow profitability in the fourth fiscal quarter, despite a massive ramp in CapEx spending. The enormous profitability of the Meta platform, and its associated apps, is therefore a core pillar of my strong buy rating. The firm's massive free cash flow creates new investment and acquisition opportunities for Meta and will likely lead to more stock buybacks in the future as well. The social media network just acquired another start-up, Manus, in order to boost its AI capabilities, which is part of a multi-billion dollar investment push into new technologies.

Previous rating

I previously rated shares of the social media company a strong buy -- Hefty Tax Charge, CapEx Ramp, Bargain P/E -- due to the platform excelling in the quest for superintelligence, investing billions of dollars into its new AI businesses, and remaining extremely free cash flow at the same time. Meta continues to have a ton of catalysts in 2026, including an aggressive CapEx plan and strong momentum in the advertising business. I like shares of Meta as a long term play on superintelligence and believe the firm's potential is not yet fully recognized.

Meta beats Q4 '25 estimates

The social media platform exceeded bottom- and top line estimates for the fourth fiscal quarter by large amounts on Wednesday: Meta reported $8.88 per-share in normalized earnings for Q4 '25, beating the consensus estimate by $0.70 per-share. The revenue figure was reported at $59.9B, out-matching the consensus prediction by $1.42B.

Meta generated a total of $59.9B in revenue in the fourth fiscal quarter, which calculates to a year-over-year growth rate of approximately 24%. Meta’s results in Q4 '25 were mainly supported by higher-than-average advertiser spending on the social media platform due to the inclusion of the Christmas holiday period. As a result, the social media enterprise saw record advertising revenues of $58.3B (+24% Y/Y) and the platform benefited from both user growth as well as higher advertising prices: in the important U.S./Canada market, as an example, advertising prices rose 9% year-over-year, which compares to a more moderate 5% growth rate in consolidated revenue.

A key strength of Meta is the firm’s free cash flow position, which traces right back to the platform’s massive amount of social media users. As of the end of the fourth-quarter, Meta had about 3.58B users, showing ~7% growth year-over-year. While Meta is adding mainly new users in non-core markets like Asia-Pacific and the rest of the world, the network is consistently growing. Strong user growth and higher holiday-related advertiser spending have led to a nice rebound of Meta’s free cash flows in Q4 '25.

In the fourth-quarter, Meta generated $14.1B in free cash flow, showing 33% Q/Q growth, despite elevated spending on investment initiatives. The free cash flow margin improved sequentially to 23.5%, but was nonetheless down Y/Y due to Meta's massive boost to its CapEx spending.

Catalysts for 2026

Meta is using its significant cash resources for investments into its superintelligence business. In December, the network acquired AI start-up Manus for more than $2.0B. Manus develops general-purpose autonomous AI agents, and the acquisition is a lever for Meta to accelerate its AI-driven growth strategy. Going forward, investors should expect more acquisitions in the AI start-up realm and an aggressive CapEx ramp.

Meta's CapEx in Q4 '25 was higher than average in Q4 '25, which is due to the social media platform escalating its investment spending: according to the firm’s guidance for FY 2026, Meta plans to spend between $115B and $135B this year, which calculates to a 73% increase vs. FY 2025.

In addition to CapEx spending and AI start-up acquisitions, the social media platform is in a good position (it has a very strong free cash flow position despite elevated CapEx) to spend more money on buybacks. The company did not buy back any shares in Q4 '25, but repurchased a total of $26.2B in the 2025 fiscal year. Since Meta is still relatively cheap vs. other big tech stocks, I believe buybacks could be a major catalyst for the social media company to drive shares to new all-time highs in 2026.

Meta Platform’s valuation vs. big tech rivals

Meta has seen a correction since it revealed a major tax charge in the third-quarter, but shares have seen signs of a revitalization lately, and the strong earnings report for Q4 could fuel a major breakout to the upside. Currently, shares of Meta are trading at a price-to-earnings ratio, based off of FY 2027 earnings, of 20.2X which makes them the cheapest of all big tech companies included in the industry group.

Despite its enormous profitability and a strong CapEx outlook, the market still underestimates Meta... in large part because the social media platform is splurging on building its AI business. In my opinion, Meta could easily trade at a ~25X P/E ratio going forward, which would also move the social media company closer to its Big Tech peers (the industry group average P/E is also 25.0X).

A ~25X P/E ratio, applied to a forward (FY 2027) consensus earnings estimate of $33.06 per-share, implies a fair value for the company's shares of $827, which I believe is entirely achievable this year. Considering Meta's enormous platform profitability, shares could even revalue higher than this stock price target: in my previous coverage, I highlighted that the social media company could be worth more than $1,000 by the end of the decade.

Risks with Meta

Meta is still highly dependent on the advertising business, which creates revenue concentration risk for investors. If the digital advertising market were to see a cyclical correction or the U.S. economy were to slide into a recession, advertisers most likely would roll back their spending on digital ads which could trigger a serious earnings and cash flow drop-off for Meta, given the lack of business diversification. What would change my mind about Meta is if the social media network were to see slowing user growth, weakening FCF margins (margins falling below 20%), or a decline in ad pricing.

Final thoughts

Meta's fourth-quarter earnings report was under the impression of strong advertising spending in the traditionally strong fourth calendar quarter, which includes the Christmas holiday. As a result, the social media platform crushed estimates and submitted a robust CapEx plan for FY 2026 that will see a massive acceleration of AI spending compared to 2025.

Most importantly, Meta is generating so much free cash flow that it has a considerable source of cash to tap into in order to finance its aggressive CapEx and acquire new companies in the realm of AI, such as Manus. I like that Meta’s shares are cheaper than those of most other big tech rivals, which translates to one of the most attractive risk/reward profiles in the industry group, in my opinion. Since the earnings report has been well-received by investors, I would not be surprised to see shares of Meta approach new all-time highs in the months ahead.

data.title: Kyivstar: An Overlooked GARP Opportunity
data.description:
I covered VEON Ltd. (VEON) last week, where I communicated a bullish outlook on the basis of continued fundamental growth. Today's analysis relates closely, as I discuss Kyivstar Group Ltd. (KYIV), a company that de-merged from VEON last year, in a perceived attempt to gain branding and operational independence.

As of September 2025, VEON still owned ~89.60% of Kyivstar, and to my understanding, it's believed that the parent will retain above 80% for the foreseeable future. Given the shareholder structure, I considered the tradable float and inter-party influence throughout my analysis, but still acknowledged Kyivstar's value as an independent listing; here's why I am bullish about Kyivstar's prospects, as a standalone vehicle.

What Does The Company Do?

Kyivstar is a telecommunications and digital services company that operates in Ukraine. The best way to define its core operations to readers would be to cite VEON's definition directly, which reads: "Kyivstar is Ukraine's leading digital operator, serving nearly 22.4 million mobile customers and over 1.1 million home internet fixed line customers as of June 30, 2025. Kyivstar provides a wide range of connectivity and digital services, including mobile and fixed line voice and data, ride-hailing, e-health, and digital TV, as well as enterprise services like big data, cloud, and cybersecurity solutions."

In simple terms, Kyivstar is a telecom company, generating value with an emphasis on market share in telecom and data services. The firm's exposure to Ukraine provides a unique situation, whereby it has tapped into an emerging market. From a market-based vantage point, Kyivstar has a market capitalisation of ~$2.87 billion; most shares are owned by VEON and institutions. That being said, I think retail demand will tick-up in the coming years, if Kyivstar brands itself successfully under its independent status.

Key Drivers

Fundamental Growth & Market Share

Kyivstar's growth plan explicitly states an emphasis on using its telecom business as a ballast via market share and sustainable income. In addition, it plans on leveraging its existing customer base into digital offerings via cross-selling, as a means to regenerate top-line growth, while shifting to a more asset-light business model; key progress metrics of each are discussed throughout this subsection.

Kyivstar's Q3 earnings quantifies much of the opening paragraph. Broad-based revenue grew by 19.8% year-over-year in USD-terms, to $297 million. Of the revenue, direct digital contributed $35 million, a 5.26x increase year-over-year. Collectively, EBITDA grew by 20.4% year-over-year to $171 million, and CapEx intensity settled at $89 million, showing that the firm's recent expansion and maintenance have developed at a noteworthy cost. The balance sheet position remains tidy; a $472 million cash position, leverage ratio of 3.2x, and implicit support from VEON provide a foundation for future scale.

Uklon Acquisition & Segmental Performance

I'd like to walk you through Kyivstar' key segments to highlight value-adds and potential detractors. However, first, I'd like to discuss Uklon, an e-hailing company, recently acquired by Kyivstar for a reported $155.2 million (97% of shares). In verbatim, Uklon's operations can be summarised as: "Uklon operates in 27 cities across Ukraine and unites more than 100 thousand driver-partners on the platform. The company facilitated over 100 million rides and more than 3 million deliveries in 2024. In 2023, Uklon entered the deliveries business and expanded its operations into Uzbekistan, where VEON's Beeline Uzbekistan also operates as the country's leading digital operator."

In essence, Uklon provides a diversified source of revenue via e-hailing, while delivering potential regional and infrastructure synergies. It has an existing market presence and seems like a combined effort at enhancing Kyivstar's financial and strategic presence (in simple terms, Kyivstar likely bought Uklon for both financial gain and synergies).

The following figure illustrates a few of Uklon's key metrics, conveying $24.7 million in total revenue during Q3 and $9.1 million in EBITDA, driven by over 3.6 million in monthly-active users and over 1.2 million rides booked. What I've garnered from these figures is Uklon isn't a fairytale; it is profitable, it has an active customer base, it's asset-light, and it's scalable-excellent opportunity.

Looking across verticals, Kyivstar has derived serious growth through direct digital revenue in pockets such as MyKyivstar and Kyivstar TV, which provide online telecom management services to individual clients and online television and media content. Total monthly active users across direct digital grew by 49.4% year-over-year, mainly due to Uklon's acquisition, but notable gains were made through Kyivstar TV and digital enterprise (which includes offerings in big data, cloud, and the internet of things).

A segment that I would like to highlight is Helsi. Helsi is a digital healthcare platform, which is exceptionally scalable. Underlying growth metrics remain quite stale, but I believe Helsi could be a low CapEx business that could develop significant value once industry consolidation has occurred. Top-line revenue grew by 50.9% year-over-year to $1.8 million, revealing solid financial gains. The reason I'm quite optimistic about Helsi is because of its diversified source of revenue, in a low-cyclicality industry.

In terms of outlook for fiscal 2025, Kyivstar's management has guided toward 20% - 23% in USD-based revenue growth and 19% - 22% EBITDA growth. CapEx intensity is set to remain, which is unsurprising, given the company's commitment to pivoting into digital offerings. Whether these figures will be re-rated in fiscal year 2026, remains to be seen - I expect continuation in the trend, on the basis of market share and ongoing support from an emerging economy, Ukraine.

Valuation and Trading Metrics

Kyivstar remains a high-growth company with substantial intangible assets and a developing capital structure. Therefore, I'd isolate three metrics, namely the price-to-earnings-growth ratio of 0.89, the forward EV/EBITDA ratio of 4.66x, and the trailing price-to-cash-flow ratio of 5.6x. The sub-1 PEG ratio conveys a growth-at-a-reasonable price possibility, while the forward EV/EBITDA ratio is 44.60% below the sector median, and the P/CF ratio conveys solid operating income (before accounting adjustments). The reasons I isolated these ratios are because, 1) I think Kyivstar provides a balance between growth and value, 2) EBITDA phases out subjective amortisation accounting and accounts for a developing capital structure, and 3) the P/CF ratio provides a sense of actual cash movement, as opposed to measuring flows on an accounting standards basis. Overall, I see good headline value in Kyivstar.

Kyivstar's RSI sits below 50, suggesting a possible entry point has emerged. An RSI above 70 would signal excess buying pressure, the stock remains far below that threshold.

Material Risk Factors

Concentrated Ownership

Concentrated ownership means Kyivstar's price can drift away from its fair value, largely due to majority shareholders buying/selling to fit their own corporate strategies. VEON derives economic value from Kyivstar, it's not a minority investor, and therefore, its treatment of buying/selling shares might be different to an investor seeking relative value.

Russia-Ukraine Exposure

Kyivstar is highly exposed to the Russia-Ukraine war, and its acquisition of Uklon intensifies its exposure. As shown by my VEON analysis, the company's stock price can be sensitive to tangible threats to the ongoing war.

Conclusion

I think Kyivstar provides an option to those seeking exposure to VEON's Ukraine operations. VEON is diversified across other EMs, such as Bangladesh and Pakistan. Kyivstar isolates a specific geography and, based on its fundamentals, has done so rather successfully.

Valuation metrics suggest the stock could be a growth-at-a-reasonable price opportunity. Though risks such as concentrated ownership and exposure to the Russia-Ukraine war exists, I'm communicating a positive outlook on Kyivstar.

data.title: Wildan Group: Grid Modernization To Sustain Current Momentum
data.description:
Introduction and Investment Thesis

I am bullish on Wildan Group, Inc. (NASDAQ: WLDN), driven by U.S. pressure to modernize its grid infrastructure amid skyrocketing power demand, especially from data centers. For example, in 2025, power grid demand reached 22%, which is far higher than the 12% projected demand for 2028.

The most interesting part is that Willdan has already taken an early foothold in this grid modernization through a $97 million contract to design and implement energy and infrastructure upgrades in 24 sites. By installing solar panels, adding electric vehicle charging stations, and electrifying heating and cooling systems (HVAC), this contract shows Wildan Group can modernize the American power grid, estimated to cut around 1,777 metric tons of carbon annually.

I believe Wildan’s one year price return of 245.77% demonstrates this competitive market positioning in grid modernization, as it beats the S&P 500 by a considerable margin at 15.60%.

Company Brief

Wildan Group, Inc. provides energy and engineering consulting services. The company operates in the energy segment, which covers grid optimization, infrastructure projects, and efficiency, while the engineering segment covers city planning, disaster recovery, safety, and compliance. The major clients include governments, industries, and schools in the United States.

Grid Modernization

From a broader perspective, the U.S. needs grid modernization to address the increase in electricity demand from around 50% between 2025 from 3,500 TWh to 2030 5,250 TWh.

Today, data centers are the leading drivers of electricity demand, followed by EVs, both of which are significant areas of projected grid modernization. The U.S. Department of Energy (DOE) is supporting research and development in grid modernization to unlock reliable, affordable, and secure energy transmission. Looking at electricity demand, it is worth noting that transmission is a significant challenge the U.S. faces in meeting its full energy demand. As such, the Federal Energy Regulatory Commission (FERC) has unanimously approved a suite of actions to modernize the security and reliability of the American Power System. In fact, the FERC has annexed grid modernization to the nation’s economic prosperity, the well-being of Americans, and national security.

In my view, with the growing demand as previously indicated and the continued government backing for grid modernization, it is now easier for companies such as Willdan Group to modernize grid transmission. One example of how I look at this is through the Bipartisan Infrastructure Law, which has provided more than $21 billion in grants specifically for grid modernization to make it easier for companies to implement.

After reviewing the company’s eligibility for this grant, I find that companies that install technologies that enhance grid flexibility are eligible for around $3 billion. Given that the distribution of this grant has been available from 2022 through 2026, at $600 million/year, this shows that in 2026, there is $600 million available for grip modernization. This indicates that Willdan Group is likely to be eligible for this funding directly or indirectly. It may not be the primary recipient of the grant funding, since it is a service provider rather than a utility or asset owner, but it has a high likelihood of receiving this grant.

Let me take another pragmatic view. Willdan Group has, since 2022, helped its customers achieve over 800 GWh in electricity savings, 612,000 mtCO2e avoided GHG emissions, and 14.7 million therms of natural gas savings through grid modernization. Taking a $97 million contract in Alameda County for an energy and infrastructure upgrade, this is a perfect example that the country is still committed to sustainable energy grid transmission. The reason is that this contract entails the installation of EV charging stations, PV generation, and other decarbonization upgrades. I derive from this that even though the current U.S. government has cut tax credits for sustainable energy production, such as PV, the competition for energy remains. For example, the U.S. government is focusing on industrial competition in 2026.

But what I am noting here is that the government has just turned energy policy into industrial policy, which is an all-inclusive idea. Looking at it through the lens of building factories, it means that the construction of wind and solar farms is inevitable. So far, China is dominating the energy race on all fronts, from clean energy sources to carbon emitter sources. But the essence here is that China’s status as the world’s industrial powerhouse reflects the fulfillment of energy demand. Therefore, as the U.S aims to achieve onshoring, modernizing its grid transmission is central to this goal. This enables Willdan Group to offer more grid modernization services.

Financials and Valuation

Over the last couple of years, WLDN revenue has been on an uptrend, which is an indication of its strong revenue momentum.

To be precise, since 2022, the company has maintained a consistent double-digit YoY growth, with the trailing YoY growth standing at 12.9%. In my view, this is an indication that the company is riding on the rising demand for grid modernization to sustain its growth.

The consensus revenue estimates for FY2025 stand at $682.30 million, suggesting that the company might record 20.6% YoY revenue growth. Based on my views on the 2026 market tailwinds, I believe the company can maintain the double-digit YoY revenue growth in the next couple of years.

In 2026, analysts are slightly conservative about Wildan Group’s revenues, forecasting up to $740 million, which is 8.45% below a double-digit figure. I think this is driven by contrasting views on 2026 GDP growth expectations, with some citing a potential slowdown to around 1.8%. I believe this is not a reason to worry, as there is a high likelihood of a rebound layer in the quarters ahead.

Moving to the bottom line, the revenue upward trajectory is reflected in net income as well. In 2022, YoY revenue growth was plummeting, and the company recorded a loss. As of 2023, the company’s net income was $10.93 million and has since increased to $41.53 million in 2025. This reflects how the company’s profitability is tied to its revenue trajectory. With the topline in a consistent upward momentum, I am confident its profitability is likely to keep improving.

If all goes as forecast, in FY2025, EPS might hit $2.86, representing 81% YoY growth. This points to a bright outlook as far as this company’s profitability is concerned, which coincides with my bullish stance, backed by the drivers discussed above.

I will now wrap up this section by valuing Willdan Group using the P/S multiple and comparing it with its peers, ICF International, Inc. (NASDAQ: ICFI) and Stantec Inc. (NYSE: STN). Willdan Group’s P/S multiple is 2.83x, which almost matches that of STN’s at 2.51x, but ICFI at 0.91x is a distant third.

In my opinion, this valuation is a reflection of the market’s optimism in this company’s growth potential and its market positioning. Looking at the forward revenue outlook for FY2026, Willdan’s revenues might reach $740 million, representing a YoY growth rate of 8.46%. Compared to STN, with projected revenue of $5.18 billion and 9.82% YoY revenue growth, Willdan is competing closely with a giant whose revenue base is diverse and I believe its market valuation is justified by this close growth potential. ICFI is a testament to this. It has higher projected revenue than Willdan at $1.92 billion, with YoY revenue growth of 2.51%. The company’s low revenue among its peers and yet a high P/S multiple indicate high growth potential, I believe, which is why I think this company’s premium valuation is justified.

Investment Risk

While reliance on government and development contracts is a significant revenue source for Willdan, accounting for around 85%, it poses a risk if government priorities change or budget cuts are actualized. Right now, the external drivers are expected to have a strong influence on energy infrastructure growth. The major drivers of this external growth are geopolitical factors such as reshoring and the U.S. commitment to maintaining control over supply chains. Also, technological factors such as AI and hyperscaler computing are major drivers. Therefore, if government priorities change, this could affect its high-yield revenue generator, which is government developments and contracts.

Conclusion

I have a bullish stance on Willdan as the company is well positioned to be among the companies participating in U.S reshoring progress through grid modernization. This is a significant revenue driver that I think has long-term reliability for upside potential.

data.title: Columbia Disciplined Core Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market overview

U.S. equities posted a gain of 2.41% in the fourth quarter, as measured by the Russell 1000 Index. The index returned 17.37% in 2025, marking the third year in a row in which it gained 15% or more, as well as the sixth of the last seven years.

The positive three-month return was primarily a function of the same factors that propelled stocks over the full year: namely, better-than-expected economic growth, robust corporate earnings results and inflation that largely remained below an annualized rate of 3%. The U.S. Federal Reserve continued to ease policy, cutting rates by a quarter point at its meetings in October and December and announcing an end to the multi-year effort to reduce the size of its balance sheet. Stocks also remained supported by ongoing excitement surrounding the artificial intelligence (AI) theme, albeit with a brief stretch of concern in November that AI-related equities were in a “bubble.”

Notably, the quarter was characterized by a broadening of leadership away from the mega-cap technology stocks that had been key drivers of market performance for most of the year. At the style level, value stocks rose 3.81% — as measured by the Russell 1000 Value Index—and outpaced the 1.12% gain for the Russell 1000 Growth Index. Small caps also produced competitive returns relative to their larger peers after lagging for the majority of 2025, with a fourth-quarter return of 2.19% for the Russell 2000 Index.
